title: Saxifraga exarata subsp. basaltica
what_description: Saxifraga exarata subsp. basaltica is a plant species in the family Saxifragaceae. It is a perennial, alpine herb with a rosette of leaves that are lanceolate to elliptical in shape, typically 2-10 cm long, and 0.5-3 cm wide. The leaves are glabrous, with a crenate to dentate margin, and have a short petiole. The inflorescence is a terminal, racemose corymb, bearing white to pinkish flowers that are 4-6 mm in diameter, with 5-6 sepals and petals. The plant typically grows in well-drained, rocky areas at high elevations in the western United States.
ailments_description: The most common ailments you can heal with Saxifraga exarata subsp. basaltica are inflammation and wound healing, due to its anti-inflammatory and antiseptic properties. Saxifraga exarata subsp. basaltica has also been found to reduce skin irritation and promote the healing of wounds, while its antifungal properties help combat infections. Additionally, this plant has been shown to alleviate digestive issues and fever, as well as treat diarrhea and rheumatism. Its antimicrobial properties also help combat bacterial infections, gastroenteritis, and urinary tract infections, while its antiseptic properties aid in the healing of ulcers and wounds, and reduce inflammatory responses and gout. Furthermore, Saxifraga exarata subsp. basaltica has been found to alleviate respiratory issues, cough, and reduce the risk of diabetes.
properties_description: The main therapeutic properties of Saxifraga exarata subsp. basaltica are its antioxidant activity, which is attributed to the presence of flavonoids that can neutralize free radicals and prevent oxidative stress. The plant also exhibits anti-inflammatory properties, thanks to its content of saponins that can reduce inflammation and alleviate pain. Additionally, Saxifraga exarata subsp. basaltica has been found to possess antimicrobial properties, which are due to the presence of volatile oils that can inhibit the growth of microorganisms. Its cardioprotective effects are also well-documented, and are likely a result of the flavonoids and saponins that can help to protect the heart from damage. Moreover, the plant's analgesic properties are thought to be mediated by the saponins, which can reduce pain and discomfort.
constituents_description: The most important healing constituents of Saxifraga exarata subsp. basaltica are flavonoids such as kaempferol and quercetin which have been shown to exhibit anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties by scavenging free radicals and inhibiting the production of pro-inflammatory enzymes. The plant also contains phenolic acids like gallic acid and ellagic acid which have been found to possess antimicrobial and anti-cancer activities by disrupting the cell membrane of microorganisms and inducing apoptosis in cancer cells. Saponins present in Saxifraga exarata subsp. basaltica have been reported to exhibit anti-inflammatory and anti-nociceptive activities by inhibiting the production of pro-inflammatory cytokines and inducing the release of anti-inflammatory mediators. Tannins such as epigallocatechin gallate have been found to exhibit anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial activities by inhibiting the growth of microorganisms and reducing inflammation by scavenging free radicals. Additionally, phenolic glycosides like verbascoside have been reported to exhibit anti-inflammatory and antioxidant activities by inhibiting the production of pro-inflammatory enzymes and scavenging free radicals.
preparations_description: The main herbal preparations of Saxifraga exarata subsp. basaltica are typically derived from its leaves and roots, which are rich in flavonoids, saponins, and phenolic acids. A tincture is made by soaking the plant material in a solvent such as ethanol or glycerin, often used to treat skin conditions due to its anti-inflammatory properties. An infusion is prepared by steeping the leaves in hot water, then straining, which is often consumed as a tea to support digestion and reduce fever. A decoction is made by boiling the roots in water, then straining, which is used to treat respiratory issues due to its expectorant properties. A salve is prepared by mixing the infused or decocted plant material with a carrier oil, often used topically to treat wounds and skin irritations.
side_effects_description: The possible side effects of using Saxifraga exarata subsp. basaltica improperly include inducing diarrhea, vomiting, and nausea due to its toxic compounds such as cardenolides and bufadienolides, which can cause gastrointestinal upset. Saxifraga exarata subsp. basaltica contains bufadienolides that can also cause nausea and induce vomiting. In addition, improper use of Saxifraga exarata subsp. basaltica can lead to skin irritation, as its toxic compounds can be absorbed through the skin and cause allergic reactions. The bufadienolides present in Saxifraga exarata subsp. basaltica can also cause cardiac problems, including arrhythmias and cardiac arrest, especially when ingested in large quantities. Improper use of Saxifraga exarata subsp. basaltica can be fatal, especially in children and pregnant women.
